questions.question_text: By when must a Maryland licensee give the 'Understanding Whom Real Estate Agents Represent' disclosure to an unrepresented buyer or seller?
questions.correct_answer: Not later than the first scheduled face-to-face contact
distractors: When a purchase contract is signed
Within 48 hours of initial phone contact
At the time of property settlement
questions.explanation: BOP §17-530 requires the written disclosure not later than the first scheduled face-to-face contact. It is owed to parties the licensee does NOT represent — for example, a seller's agent gives it to an unrepresented buyer — and is not required for a party who already has a written brokerage agreement with a broker.

questions.question_text: A listing agent schedules a Saturday showing with an unrepresented buyer but does not present the agency disclosure form until the buyer writes an offer a week later. What violation occurred?
questions.correct_answer: Disclosure was not made by the first scheduled face-to-face contact
distractors: No violation occurred because the buyer eventually received the form
The agent should have mailed the form within 48 hours of the first call
Phone conversations exempt agents from the disclosure requirement
questions.explanation: Under BOP §17-530, a seller's agent must make the written agency disclosure to an unrepresented buyer not later than the first scheduled face-to-face contact — here, the Saturday showing. Waiting until the offer was written misses the statutory deadline.

questions.question_text: What is the primary purpose of Maryland's brokerage disclosure form?
questions.correct_answer: Explain the available relationship types to clients
distractors: Establish a binding agency agreement
Disclose the commission rate to be charged
Transfer liability from the broker to the agent
questions.explanation: The brokerage disclosure form explains the types of brokerage relationships available in Maryland. It is informational, not a binding agreement, and helps consumers make informed decisions about representation.

questions.question_text: An agent holds a listed property open to the public. How can the agent satisfy Maryland's agency disclosure requirement for open-house visitors?
questions.correct_answer: Conspicuously display a notice stating whom the agent represents
distractors: Verbally announce the agency relationship to each visitor
Collect a signed disclosure form from every visitor before entry
No disclosure obligation can arise at an open house
questions.explanation: BOP §17-530 provides that a licensee holding a property open to the public complies with the agency disclosure requirement by conspicuously displaying a notice indicating whom the licensee represents. Outside the open-house setting, the written 'Understanding Whom Real Estate Agents Represent' form must be given to unrepresented parties not later than the first scheduled face-to-face contact; a purely verbal explanation never suffices.

questions.question_text: An agent emails listing information to a prospect for two weeks before they meet in person. At the meeting, the agent forgets the disclosure form. The agent argues disclosure was unnecessary since the relationship began online. Is this correct?
questions.correct_answer: No, disclosure is required at the first face-to-face meeting
distractors: Yes, the online relationship satisfies disclosure rules
Yes, if the emails included the agent's license number
Only if the prospect requested the form in writing
questions.explanation: The written disclosure obligation is triggered not later than the first scheduled face-to-face contact with an unrepresented party, regardless of prior email or phone communication (BOP §17-530). Online exchanges do not substitute for the required written disclosure.

questions.question_text: Which of the following is a recognized brokerage relationship type in Maryland?
distractors: Transaction coordinator
Facilitator
Designated intermediary
questions.explanation: BOP §17-528 defines the Maryland brokerage roles: seller's agent, subagent of the seller, buyer's agent, dual agent (the broker or designated branch office manager), and intra-company agent. Terms like facilitator, transaction coordinator, or designated intermediary are not recognized Maryland relationship types.

questions.question_text: Two agents at the same Maryland brokerage work on opposite sides of a transaction — one with the seller and one with the buyer. Under Maryland law, what role do these two individual agents hold?
questions.correct_answer: Intra-company agents designated by the broker acting as dual agent
distractors: Dual agents, because they share one brokerage
Subagents of the seller
Facilitators with no fiduciary duties
questions.explanation: This situation is Maryland dual agency: the broker (or a designated branch office manager) is the dual agent, and BOP §17-530.1 requires the dual agent to assign one affiliated agent as the seller's intra-company agent and another as the buyer's intra-company agent. The individual agents are never themselves the dual agent.

questions.question_text: In Maryland, who may act as the dual agent when both the buyer and the seller in one transaction are represented through the same brokerage?
questions.correct_answer: The broker or a designated branch office manager
distractors: The individual salesperson working with both parties
Either of the two intra-company agents
Any licensee the parties jointly select
questions.explanation: Since October 1, 2016, Maryland defines the dual agent as the licensed real estate broker — or a branch office manager designated by the broker (BOP §§17-528, 17-530.1). A single salesperson may not represent both buyer and seller; with the written consent of both parties, the dual agent assigns separate intra-company agents to serve each side.

questions.question_text: A listing agent owes fiduciary duties exclusively to the seller. What type of brokerage relationship exists?
distractors: Dual agent
Intra-company agent
Buyer's agent
questions.explanation: A seller's agent represents and owes fiduciary duties exclusively to the seller. This is the most straightforward agency relationship, with clear loyalty to one party in the transaction.

questions.question_text: A Maryland broker serving as dual agent learns the buyer will pay more than the listed price. The seller asks whether the buyer would go higher. How should the dual agent respond?
questions.correct_answer: Decline to share that confidential information
distractors: Disclose the information to serve the seller
Suggest the seller raise the price without explanation
Withdraw from the transaction immediately
questions.explanation: A dual agent (the broker or designated branch office manager) may not disclose either party's confidential information without that party's written consent (BOP §17-530.1). A buyer's willingness to pay a higher price is expressly protected confidential information.

questions.question_text: A buyer chats with the listing agent at an open house and has signed no brokerage agreement. Under Maryland law, whom does that agent represent?
questions.correct_answer: The seller — the buyer is unrepresented
distractors: The buyer, as a presumed buyer's agent
Both parties, as a dual agent by default
No one, until a purchase contract is signed
questions.explanation: Maryland repealed its presumed buyer's agent rule effective October 1, 2016 (former BOP §17-533). The MREC disclosure form now states: 'If you are viewing a property and you have not signed a Brokerage Agreement, that agent represents the seller.' Buyer representation is created only by a written brokerage agreement.

questions.question_text: What duties does a Maryland licensee owe a buyer who has NOT signed a written brokerage agreement?
questions.correct_answer: Honest and fair treatment, truthful answers, and disclosure of material facts
distractors: The same fiduciary duties as a buyer's agent
Undivided loyalty and confidentiality
No duties of any kind
questions.explanation: Without a written brokerage agreement there is no buyer representation in Maryland. The licensee — acting for the seller's side — must still treat all parties honestly and fairly, answer questions truthfully, and disclose material facts about the property (BOP §17-532; MREC disclosure form). Performing ministerial acts for the buyer does not create a brokerage relationship.

questions.question_text: How is a buyer's-agency relationship created in Maryland?
questions.correct_answer: Only by a written brokerage agreement
distractors: Automatically, once the agent shows the buyer a property
By oral agreement, confirmed in writing within 30 days
By registering the relationship with the MREC
questions.explanation: A Maryland 'brokerage agreement' is by definition written (BOP §17-528), and since October 1, 2016 there is no presumed or implied buyer agency. Showing homes is providing brokerage services, so a compliant written agreement — including compensation and termination terms required by §17-534 — must be in place before an agent represents a buyer.

questions.question_text: A buyer asks an agent to show several properties but refuses to sign any written buyer brokerage agreement. What is the agent's status under Maryland law?
questions.correct_answer: The agent does not represent the buyer and works for the seller's side
distractors: Presumed buyer's agent with full buyer duties
Neutral facilitator with equal duties to both parties
Automatic dual agent for any resulting transaction
questions.explanation: Since Maryland's 2016 repeal of presumed buyer agency, an agent assisting a buyer without a written brokerage agreement represents the seller (as seller's agent or subagent). The agent must give the unrepresented buyer the 'Understanding Whom Real Estate Agents Represent' form not later than the first scheduled face-to-face contact; if the buyer will not sign, the agent certifies on the form that disclosure was made.

questions.question_text: Why did Maryland eliminate the 'presumed buyer's agent' rule effective October 1, 2016?
questions.correct_answer: So representation depends on clear written brokerage agreements
distractors: To increase commission income for buyer agents
To eliminate the need for agency disclosure forms
To comply with federal fair housing regulations
questions.explanation: The 2016 overhaul of Maryland's brokerage relationship law deleted presumed buyer agency (former BOP §17-533) so consumers know exactly whom an agent represents: buyer representation now exists only under a written brokerage agreement, unrepresented parties receive the written 'Understanding Whom Real Estate Agents Represent' disclosure, and open houses require a posted representation notice.
